_comment: The Feed is a user-generated stream. In production this is a Postgres table / API, not a static file — represented here to define item shapes. One composer creates any type. Shared 'media' array lets ANY type attach an image or diagram. Ordered socially (recency + engagement + topics), never by framework. frameworkRef is an optional tag/bridge.

feed.title: On the cache you forget
feed.body: The bug that cost us a weekend: we purged the CDN on every publish but never the Dispatcher. Stale pages lived one tier down for hours. Now every purge is instrumented end-to-end — if a tier doesn't acknowledge, the deploy fails. Invalidate top-down, prove every tier was hit.

feed.title: How I draw the cache topology in interviews
feed.body: Five boxes, top to bottom, one arrow each. If a candidate can name the invalidation strategy per tier, they understand caching. If they only know 'Redis', they've used a cache but never owned one.

media.source: graph TD; Browser-->CDN; CDN-->Dispatcher; Dispatcher-->Redis; Redis-->DB
media.alt: Five caching tiers top to bottom

feed.title: My pre-deploy checklist, 6 lines
feed.intro: Pinned above my desk. Nothing ships until all six are green.

feed.title: Why deployment has ten layers
feed.hook: 45 seconds on the request that travels DNS to database — and the layer most teams forget.

feed.prompt: A release broke prod at 3am. Rolling back means a hotfix branch, a rebuild, and a 40-minute deploy. What does this tell you?
options: The release process is fine, just bad luck
Rollback isn't a first-class operation — that's the real defect
feed.reveal: If rolling back is harder than rolling forward, the system isn't safe. Rollback should be a button press — re-promoting the last good artefact — not a hotfix branch and a rebuild.

feed.definition: Serve the cached copy immediately, refresh in the background. Hides revalidation latency from the user — the most underused cache directive on the modern web.

feed.title: Build once, promote many
feed.teaser: The same artefact moves through every environment. Rebuilding per environment is how staging and prod silently drift apart.

feed.title: On the T-minus board
feed.body: The one ritual that cut our go-live chaos: a shared board updated daily in the final two weeks, every open item with one named owner and a date. Nothing says team or TBD. Status is always current, so the status meeting shrinks to ten minutes.
